collections.title: The Iranian Revolution
collections.summary: The series opens by establishing the deep structural foundations — the thousand-year duel between clergy and crown, the Safavid settlement, the Usuli triumph — that made clerical revolutionary leadership possible. It then traces how the Pahlavi modernization project systematically created every condition necessary for its own destruction.

episodes.title: The Thousand-Year Duel
episodes.summary: On February 1st, 1979, Ayatollah Khomeini descended the stairs of an Air France Boeing 747 at Mehrabad Airport in Tehran. Behind him lay fifteen years of exile. Ahead lay the unmaking of a dynasty that had ruled for nearly 2,500 years. But to understand how a religious scholar in a black turban toppled the Peacock Throne, we have to go back much further — to 1501, when another revolution began, one that would shape Iran’s soul for five centuries.
episodes.description: The episode establishes the series’ global question by tracing the deep structural foundations of Shi’ite clerical autonomy. Beginning with the Safavid imposition of Shi’ism in 1501 and the creation of a clerical class both dependent on and rival to royal power, the episode moves through the Usuli triumph over the Akhbaris (which established the doctrinal foundation for clerical independence), the Qajar dualism that represented a stable equilibrium, and the Constitutional Revolution of 1906 that first revealed the fault line between parliamentary sovereignty and Islamic law.

episodes.title: The Modernizer’s Trap
episodes.summary: The Shah left Iran on January 16th, 1979, bewildered and broken. But the trap that caught him didn’t spring overnight — it was built, piece by piece, by his own father, then inherited and amplified by him. Today, we trace how modernization itself became the engine of revolution.
episodes.description: The episode traces the self-defeating logic of Pahlavi modernization, showing how father and son pursued nearly identical paradigms that simultaneously transformed Iran and generated the resentments that fueled opposition.

episodes.title: The Foundational Trauma
episodes.summary: A revolution is not made by revolutionaries — it is made by the ancien régime destroying itself. Today, we trace how external powers gave that trap its final, fatal mechanism.
episodes.description: The episode examines the 1953 coup as the foundational trauma that connects Iran’s democratic aspirations to revolutionary radicalism.

episodes.title: The Invisible Revolution
episodes.summary: A revolution is not made by revolutionaries. It is made by the people. But how do ordinary people become revolutionary? Today, we trace the invisible networks that transformed seeds into an unstoppable force.
episodes.description: The episode reveals the invisible revolution — the intellectual currents and organizational networks that were building beneath the surface while both the Shah and America remained blind.

episodes.title: The Year of Living Dangerously
episodes.summary: On February 11, 1979, the Shah’s generals stopped fighting. Not because they were defeated — but because they finally understood what Khomeini had understood all along: the revolution wasn’t a military problem. It was a spiritual one.
episodes.description: The episode traces the revolutionary year of 1978 as a cascade of escalation, paralysis, and strategic ambiguity.

episodes.title: The Revolution Devours Its Children
episodes.summary: The revolution devours its children. But does it? Or do the children devour themselves, and blame the revolution?
episodes.description: The episode follows the revolution’s aftermath: the fragile coalition’s fracture, the hostage crisis as a constitutional coup, and the systematic elimination of moderates.

episodes.title: The Crucible
episodes.summary: The Iranian Revolution cannot be reduced to a single cause, a single actor, or a single framework. And that irreducibility is not a failure of analysis — it is the deepest truth about what happened.
episodes.description: The final episode examines the revolution’s crucible and legacy: the Iran-Iraq War, the 1988 mass executions, Montazeri’s dissent, and the transformation of the Sunni-Shia divide into a geopolitical fault line.

collections.title: The Partition of India
collections.summary: The series begins by asking whether partition was a failure of individuals or of structures, then moves through the specific mechanisms — colonial engineering, electoral mobilization, the politics of speed, the drawing of borders, the organization of violence — showing how each mechanism transformed contingency into catastrophe.

episodes.title: The Seeds of Division
episodes.summary: Establishes the structural foundations of partition: British divide-and-rule, separate electorates, and the long arc from Hindu-Muslim unity to its collapse.

episodes.title: The Ambassador Becomes the Architect
episodes.summary: Traces Jinnah’s transformation and the shifting meaning of ‘Pakistan,’ staging the debate between Congress hubris and Jinnah’s intransigence.

episodes.title: The Last Exit
episodes.summary: Examines the Cabinet Mission as the final alternative to partition, Nehru’s fatal press conference, and the question of inevitability.

episodes.title: The Roulette Wheel of August 15
episodes.summary: Focuses on Mountbatten’s acceleration, the Radcliffe Line, and the administrative absurdity of dividing the indivisible in 73 days.

episodes.title: The Violence Beneath
episodes.summary: Investigates strategic violence, Sikh militancy as the unforeseen third force, and the mass migration that became an unplanned referendum.

episodes.title: The Prophet and the Assassin
episodes.summary: Follows Gandhi’s marginalization and moral authority, his fasts, the Nehru-Patel rift, and his assassination as paradoxical stabilizer.

episodes.title: Partition’s Infinite Recursion
episodes.summary: Traces how partition’s logic reproduced itself in the princely states, Kashmir, and the hardening of borders into nuclear standoff.

episodes.title: The Furies Unloosed
episodes.summary: Weaves together the series’ threads to reveal that partition did not resolve the communal question but relocated it.

collections.title: The Question of Palestine
collections.summary: The series opens by establishing the conflict’s foundational paradox: how a movement born from persecution became an instrument of dispossession.

episodes.title: A Land Without a People
episodes.summary: Establishes the foundational erasure and colonial logic.

episodes.title: The Catastrophe
episodes.summary: Was 1948 premeditated ethnic cleansing or wartime chaos?

episodes.title: The Iron Wall
episodes.summary: How every supposed neutral broker was structurally aligned with one side.

episodes.title: The Myth of Existential Threat
episodes.summary: 1967 and the gap between public rhetoric and private planning.

episodes.title: The Resistance Paradox
episodes.summary: The structural trap that made every strategy simultaneously necessary and counterproductive.

episodes.title: The Mirage of Peace
episodes.summary: Oslo from historic breakthrough to Palestinian Versailles.

episodes.title: The Laboratory
episodes.summary: How occupation became a globally exportable product.

episodes.title: The Moral Labyrinth
episodes.summary: Two peoples, one land, no clean exit.

collections.title: The War on Terror
collections.summary: The series builds a single causal argument: from 1979 to the rise of ISIS, decisions that seemed rational in isolation created feedback loops of radicalization, proxy warfare, occupation failure, and state collapse. Across Afghanistan, Pakistan, Iraq, and Syria, the same structures kept reproducing worse outcomes.

episodes.title: The Year That Broke Everything
episodes.summary: Traces 1979 through the Iranian Revolution, the siege of Mecca, and the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan, showing how one year created the feedback loops that shaped everything after.
episodes.description: The episode frames 1979 as the hinge year that launched the Saudi-Iranian rivalry, global jihad infrastructure, and Zia's Islamization of Pakistan, establishing the series thesis that later catastrophes were chained consequences rather than isolated surprises.

episodes.title: The Factory of Jihad
episodes.summary: Follows the CIA-ISI covert war, Saudi funding, and the Peshawar nexus that built the physical and human infrastructure of the jihadist international.
episodes.description: The episode examines Afghanistan's covert war as an institutional system with no off switch, asking whether transnational jihadism was merely enabled or actively constructed by the states that armed and organized it.

episodes.title: The Idea That Ate the World
episodes.summary: Maps the ideological chain from Sayyid Qutb through Azzam and Zawahiri to bin Laden, showing how jihad was transformed into operational doctrine.
episodes.description: The episode traces how theological concepts became recruitment tools, military strategy, and a transnational worldview, tying Saudi Wahhabism and bin Laden's radicalization into the intellectual architecture of mass violence.

episodes.title: The Blind Watchmen
episodes.summary: Reconstructs the intelligence failures, missed opportunities, and alliance compromises that allowed 9/11 despite repeated warnings.
episodes.description: The episode follows the CIA-FBI wall, failed chances to stop bin Laden, Massoud's assassination, and the suppression of moderate alternatives to ask whether 9/11 was a failure of imagination or of institutions.

episodes.title: The Unmaking of Iraq
episodes.summary: Shows how sanctions, invasion, de-Baathification, army dissolution, and sectarian civil war destroyed Iraq in stages, each step preparing the next.
episodes.description: The episode tracks Iraq's destruction from the sanctions era through Bremer's decrees and the Samarra bombing to examine whether sectarian collapse was structurally baked into the invasion or intensified by catastrophic postwar decisions.

episodes.title: The Occupation Trap
episodes.summary: Pulls back from individual theaters to reveal the repeating intervention pattern linking Afghanistan, Iraq, Libya, and Syria.
episodes.description: The episode argues that foreign occupation, torture, proxy patronage, and media amnesia repeatedly generated the resistance they claimed to suppress, raising the question of whether the lesson is to intervene better or not at all.

episodes.title: The Caliphate and the Reckoning
episodes.summary: Brings the whole chain to its endpoint in ISIS, showing the caliphate as the convergence of earlier wars, ideological shifts, and state failures.
episodes.description: The final episode connects Zarqawi, Maliki, Assad, Gulf funding, and Wahhabist ideology to the collapse of coexistence and the rise of ISIS, ending on the unresolved question of whether the post-1979 system can still be broken.
